title: Strait of Hormuz Ceasefire Unravels as Diesel Crisis Deepens, Japan Calls Capital Home
subhead: The US-Iran memorandum of understanding buckled this week under renewed tanker attacks and fresh strikes, Russia banned diesel exports tightening a global fuel crunch, and Japan's pivot to redirect its $1.8 trillion pension fund toward domestic assets sent the yen surging — a day that underscored how military conflict and monetary policy are colliding across multiple fronts.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz Ceasefire Unravels as Iran Attacks Tankers
sections.body:
The US-Iran memorandum of understanding, signed just three weeks ago with a 60-day ceasefire, has buckled under its own contradictions. Iran attacked three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz this week, targeting ships using a US-protected southern corridor along Oman's coast — a route that bypasses Iran's territorial waters. The US revoked the general license authorizing Iranian crude sales and launched new strikes against Iran in response. Oil prices rose more than 4% this week, with US crude around $71 per barrel and Brent just under $76 — still well below wartime highs near $122.

The fundamental dispute is over transit routes. Iran insists the MOU's safe-passage provisions only apply to ships using a northern route through Iranian waters, while the US military has been escorting vessels through the southern corridor. CNBC reports that the deal's fifth paragraph says Iran 'will make arrangements using its best efforts' to ensure safe passage — but specific routes were left undefined. David Goldwyn, former State Department energy envoy, told CNBC: 'We're back to where we were before the memorandum of understanding.' Iran's Revolutionary Guard warned Thursday that US military 'interference in determining the routes of maritime traffic' would be met with a 'decisive response.'

The US is now demanding Iran publicly declare the Strait of Hormuz open and commit to not attacking commercial ships. Iran's Foreign Ministry called the reinstated oil sanctions a 'material breach' of the MOU. The IEA warned Friday that an escalation of hostilities could upend its forecast of a significant oil market surplus in 2027, noting that global supply jumped in June when Hormuz reopened but still lagged pre-war levels.
sections.whyItMatters: The MOU's collapse leaves the world's most critical oil chokepoint in a gray zone — neither open nor closed — with Iran trying to assert control over all shipping while lacking the military capacity to close the strait entirely. The precedent matters: if Iran succeeds in taxing transit, other chokepoints could face similar coercion.

sections.heading: Russia Bans Diesel Exports, Deepening Global Fuel Crisis
sections.body:
Russia's decision to ban diesel exports this week has roiled global energy markets, sending prices soaring even in countries that no longer buy Russian fuel. Reuters reports that Russian diesel and gas oil loadings fell to 234,000 barrels per day from July 1-10, down from 400,000 bpd in June and the 2025 average of around 817,000 bpd. The ban was driven by domestic shortages caused by Ukrainian drone strikes on refineries — Ukraine announced this week it is creating a dedicated 'long-range impact' command to step up such attacks.

The impact is global. US diesel futures posted their biggest daily gains in four years on Wednesday. US government data showed a draw of more than 4.5 million barrels of diesel last week, bringing inventories to 97.8 million barrels — 6% below the five-year average. SCMP reports that the price spikes are threatening agriculture, power, and transport sectors across Europe and the US. Russia is the world's second-largest diesel exporter after the US, and the loss of its supply comes on top of the Hormuz disruption.

This is a two-front fuel crisis: the Middle East constrains crude oil flows while the Russia-Ukraine war constrains refined product supply. Reuters notes that gasoline and diesel markets have been signalling a supply crunch even with relatively subdued crude prices, suggesting the energy shock from the Iran war is far from over in the products that actually power the global economy.
sections.whyItMatters: The diesel crunch is more immediately damaging than crude price moves. Diesel powers trucks, ships, farming equipment, and backup generators — and spot shortages could hit food prices and industrial output faster than oil market abstracts suggest. The convergence of two wars squeezing different parts of the fuel supply chain is unprecedented.

sections.heading: Japan Calls Capital Home: GPIF Pivot and BOJ Inflation Pressure
sections.body:
Japan's Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama announced Friday that the government aims to steer the Government Pension Investment Fund — the world's largest pension fund at roughly $1.8 trillion — to 'substantially' increase investment in domestic assets. The yen immediately bounced, with USD/JPY falling 0.6% to 161.44. Japan's 10-year government bond yield dropped 3.4%. The move mirrors a similar shift by South Korea in December and, according to Reuters, represents an attempt to reverse the Abe-era capital outflows that have suppressed the yen for years.

The timing is no accident. Japan's wholesale prices rose 7.1% year-on-year in June, the fastest pace in over three years, according to BOJ data released Friday. Naphtha surged 81.6%, petroleum products climbed 22.8%, and import prices jumped 29.7% — the largest gain since October 2022 — driven by the weak yen and Middle East conflict. The BOJ raised its policy rate to a 31-year high of 1.0% in June, and sources told Reuters the bank may revise up its growth forecast while maintaining vigilance on inflation risks.

Corporate bankruptcies hit a 12-year high of over 5,300 in H1 2026, according to Business Standard, as inflation and labor costs squeeze small businesses. The GPIF pivot is both a yen-support measure and an industrial policy: PM Takaichi wants domestic capital flowing into AI and semiconductor investment, not overseas equities.
sections.whyItMatters: If Japan's $1.8 trillion pension fund begins reallocating meaningfully toward domestic assets, the ripple effects across global markets would be enormous — Japanese institutions are among the largest foreign holders of US Treasuries and European debt. Domestically, the move could finally strengthen the yen, reduce import cost pressure, and fund Takaichi's industrial ambitions — but it also risks inflating Japanese asset bubbles.

sections.heading: US Bans CBDC as Housing Bill Becomes Law Without Trump's Signature
sections.body:
A four-year ban on a US central bank digital currency will become law at midnight Friday, embedded in Congress' bipartisan housing-affordability bill. President Trump refused to sign the legislation, using a pocket-veto protest to demand passage of a separate elections bill — but under the US Constitution, the bill becomes law after 10 days whether signed or not, since Trump did not formally veto it.

The CBDC restriction blocks the Federal Reserve from issuing a digital dollar through the end of 2030. CoinDesk reports that this was a priority for Republicans who have framed CBDCs as a surveillance threat. In practice, the Fed under Chair Kevin Warsh has shown little appetite for a digital dollar, and congressional authorization was always considered a prerequisite. But the ban creates an asymmetric global landscape: the European Parliament cleared the final step for a digital euro in late June, and China continues to expand its e-CNY pilot. The US is now the only major economy to have legislatively prohibited CBDC development.
sections.whyItMatters: While the practical impact is limited — the Fed wasn't pursuing a CBDC — the symbolic impact is real. The US has formally opted out of the global CBDC race at a time when Europe and China are advancing digital currency infrastructure. This affects the future of cross-border payments architecture and gives Europe and China a head start in building alternatives to dollar-denominated payment rails.

sections.heading: Senate Strikes Bipartisan Deal on Russia Sanctions Bill
sections.body:
A bipartisan group of senators announced Friday an agreement with the Trump White House on language for the long-stalled Russia sanctions bill. Senators Lindsey Graham (R-SC), Richard Blumenthal (D-CT), Jeanne Shaheen (D-NH), and Roger Wicker (R-MS) said in a statement they expect to 'roll out the legislation very soon.' The bill, first introduced in 2025, would impose 500% tariffs on countries purchasing Russian petroleum and uranium and add 500% tariffs on Russian goods imported to the US.

The deal follows the House passing Ukraine military aid and Russia sanctions legislation in June, with six Republican votes. A prior push in December was blocked by Democrats over concerns about granting the president tariff authorities during a pending Supreme Court case. The renewed effort comes as Trump has shifted toward supporting Ukraine, telling reporters alongside Zelensky at the NATO summit that the US would permit Ukraine to co-produce air defense interceptors.

Separately, Canada announced new Russia sanctions at the G7 targeting the shadow fleet and energy revenues. The UK seized a Russian shadow fleet oil tanker in the English Channel and also announced new sanctions. The EU adopted its 21st sanctions package in June, targeting energy revenues, the military-industrial complex, and propaganda — though Bulgaria announced it would veto the package over economic concerns.
sections.whyItMatters: If the 500% tariff regime passes, it would dramatically escalate secondary sanctions on Russian energy buyers — primarily China and India. This would force those countries to choose between cheap Russian oil and access to the US market, a calculation that could reshape global energy trade flows. The fact that the White House signed off on the language is a significant shift in Trump's Russia posture.

sections.heading: G7 Flashpoints: Trump's 100% Wine Tariff Threat, Digital Tax Escalation
sections.body:
The G7 summit in Evian has been marked by a transatlantic trade confrontation. Trump threatened a 100% tariff on French wine and champagne unless President Macron drops France's 3% digital services tax, which has been in effect since 2019 and targets US tech giants including Apple, Amazon, Google, and Meta. Trump expanded the threat Friday to a 100% tariff on any country that implements a digital services tax on US tech firms. Macron rebuked Trump publicly, stating 'the US does not decide European law.'

French wine and spirits exports to the US already face a 15% tariff and slumped 21% last year, according to the French Federation of Wine and Spirits Exporters. The US is France's largest export market for wines and spirits, accounting for 21% of the total. Canada scrapped its own digital services tax last year under similar US pressure during trade negotiations.

Beyond the US-France clash, Japan's PM Takaichi pushed a mineral stockpiling plan at the G7 and sought to bridge divides on China trade tactics, while surging Chinese exports to Europe raised alarm among European leaders. The summit also discussed granting trusted partners access to cutting-edge US AI models — another front where technology policy is becoming trade policy.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump is weaponizing tariff threats to kill digital services taxes globally — a direct challenge to how sovereign nations tax multinational tech platforms. If 100% tariffs on wine become the price of keeping US tech firms untaxed, other countries with similar digital tax proposals (UK, Italy, Spain, Austria) will face the same pressure. This is trade policy as regulatory policy.

sections.heading: Fed Maintains Hawkish Stance as Inflation Risks Persist
sections.body:
Minutes from the Federal Reserve's June meeting revealed policymakers remained divided over the need for additional interest rate hikes, though the leanest readout of a Fed meeting in years still made clear that inflation concerns remain elevated. Reuters reports that Fed officials 'fret over inflation risk' and are weighing further rate hikes despite geopolitical uncertainty. Markets are pricing in a significant probability of additional tightening through 2026.

The conflicting pressures are stark. Energy costs from the Iran conflict and diesel crunch push inflation up, while softer payroll data and the possibility of a Hormuz resolution pull in the other direction. Gold was headed for a weekly decline on Friday as rate-hike expectations weighed, with concerns that escalating Gulf attacks reinforce the case for tighter policy. Bernstein updated its gold price target downward following the Fed's rate shift.

Fed Chair Kevin Warsh, who has advocated for fewer press conferences and less forward guidance, is facing his first full meeting with the dual challenge of war-driven commodity inflation and a domestic economy showing mixed signals. June CPI data, due next week, will be a critical data point. CNBC notes that investors are bracing for a week where earnings season and inflation data converge with the reassertion of US-Iran hostilities.
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed's path is increasingly constrained by external shocks. If the Hormuz crisis escalates and diesel shortages push consumer prices higher, the central bank may be forced into rate hikes it would prefer to avoid in a slowing labor market. The combination of supply-side inflation and demand-side weakness is the classic stagflation trap — and Warsh's hawkish instincts could make the response sharper than markets expect.

bottomLine: Three overlapping crises converged this week: the US-Iran ceasefire collapsed back into tanker attacks and military strikes in the Strait of Hormuz, Russia's diesel export ban squeezed an already tight global fuel market, and Japan made an unprecedented pivot to bring $1.8 trillion of pension capital home. At the G7, Trump threatened 100% tariffs on French wine to kill digital services taxes worldwide, while the US Senate finally struck a bipartisan deal on 500% secondary tariffs on Russian energy buyers. With the Fed holding hawkish, the BOJ facing 7.1% wholesale inflation, and a CBDC ban becoming US law by default, the institutional architecture of global finance is shifting on multiple fronts at once.
sources: CNBC, Reuters, SCMP, CoinDesk, The Hill, France24, Mainichi/Kyodo, EconoTimes, Business Standard, Bloomberg, AP, AFP
